transcript:
Voice note to self - November 22nd, late evening.

Just got off a call with CloudScale. Lost the deal. They went with BetterUp. I'm frustrated but also confused.

Two months ago, Jennifer and David were both excited. David literally did the ROI math on the call and it made sense. Jennifer called out our tech-specific coaching as the differentiator over BetterUp.

What changed? David's email was vague. Said they 'decided to go in a different direction' and BetterUp 'offered a more comprehensive solution.' What does that even mean?

I'm replaying the last two months. We sent the proposal on time. Followed up twice. They went dark for three weeks, which should have been a red flag. When I finally reconnected with David in early December, he said they were 'still evaluating.' Then today, the rejection.

My hypothesis: we got outmaneuvered on features or price. BetterUp probably bundled in manager training, maybe wellness, mental health, other stuff we don't offer. Made us look narrow even though our tech-specific coaching was what they wanted.

Or maybe it was price. $700k annual is real money. BetterUp might have discounted aggressively. Or offered a smaller pilot.

Or maybe I lost Jennifer. She was the HR stakeholder. BetterUp is the name brand in HR circles. Maybe her peers or her CEO questioned going with a smaller vendor. I never got to meet the CEO or CFO. Was that the miss?

What would I do differently? 

First, I should have asked for more stakeholder meetings. Just David and Jennifer wasn't enough. Needed the CEO or at least the CFO who controls the budget.

Second, when they went dark for three weeks, I should have been more aggressive. One email per week wasn't enough. Should have called David directly.

Third, I never asked about their evaluation process. Who else are they looking at? What's their timeline? What's their decision criteria? I assumed it was just us and maybe one other vendor. Clearly BetterUp was in the mix the whole time.

Fourth, the proposal might have been too narrow. We focused on engineering career coaching. Maybe we should have positioned it more broadly - leadership development, team effectiveness, manager training. Made it feel bigger.

Bigger lesson: I keep optimizing for the perfect pitch and the perfect product. But enterprise deals are about relationships, politics, and navigating bureaucracy. I'm a founder who loves product and coaching. I need to get better at the political game.

Also, losing deals to BetterUp specifically is a pattern. This is the third time this year. They have the brand, the sales machine, the feature breadth. We have better coaching quality for engineers. But quality alone isn't winning.

What do I do with this? 

Short term: I need to debrief with David or Jennifer if they'll take the call. Understand what actually happened. What BetterUp offered that we didn't. What our weaknesses were.

Medium term: We need to revisit our sales playbook. How do we compete against BetterUp when they show up? Do we need to add more features? Better pricing? Different positioning?

Long term: Maybe this is forcing me to think about partnerships or acquisitions. Could we partner with a manager training company? A wellness provider? Make ourselves feel more comprehensive?

Or maybe this is just part of being a startup. We'll lose deals to incumbents. The key is winning enough deals with companies who value our differentiation.

Okay, I'm spiraling. Time to stop. Tomorrow I'll reach out to David for a debrief call. Then I'll sit down with the team and figure out what changes we need to make.

For tonight, I'm going to acknowledge I'm frustrated and disappointed. This one hurt. We put two months into it. It felt winnable. And we lost.

Tomorrow we'll learn from it and get better.

End note.
